target.title: fix(memory-lancedb): reject envelope metadata sludge from auto-capture and recall
target.body:
fix(memory-lancedb): reject envelope metadata sludge from auto-capture and recall

## Summary

OpenClaw injects envelope scaffolding into user-role messages before they reach the AI:

- `Conversation info (untrusted metadata):` + JSON block
- `Sender (untrusted metadata):` + JSON block
- `Thread starter (untrusted, for context):`
- `Replied message (untrusted, for context):`
- `Forwarded message context (untrusted metadata):`
- `Chat history since last reply (untrusted, for context):`
- `active-turn-recovery` boilerplate
- `[media attached: ...]` claim-check annotations
- Leading timestamp prefixes like `[Mon 2026-04-14 12:34 EDT]`

The `memory-lancedb` extension's `shouldCapture` and auto-capture pipeline had no guard against these patterns. When envelope text co-occurred with a MEMORY_TRIGGER word (e.g., "always", "prefer", an email address in a sender block), the entire contaminated message was vectorized and stored as a long-term memory. Contaminated memories were also injected back into recall context on subsequent turns. This poisoned future recall and caused traced production failures where a dispatched subagent worked the wrong task because a poisoned memory matched its context.

## Changes

- `looksLikeEnvelopeSludge(text)`: predicate that detects any of the known envelope/transport metadata patterns. Uses line-anchored matching for the `Untrusted context (metadata...)` header to avoid false-positives on user content that quotes the phrase mid-sentence. Uses compound-key narrowing for the JSON-blob regex (e.g. `conversation_info`, `sender_name`, `channel_id`) rather than bare prefixes that could collide with legitimate user JSON.

- `sanitizeForMemoryCapture(text)`: strips envelope blocks from a message before `shouldCapture` sees it. Handles both sentinel + code-fence blocks and bare sentinel lines. Also strips `[media attached: ...]` annotations, `<active_memory_plugin>` blocks, leading timestamp prefixes, and the `Untrusted context (metadata...)` trailer. Includes a 10 000-char pre-truncation as a ReDoS mitigation.

- `escapeMemoryForPrompt`: updated to strip `[media attached: ...]` annotations before HTML-escaping so that `detectImageReferences()` cannot re-parse stored memory text as live media references.

- `agent_end` auto-capture path: sanitize each user message with `sanitizeForMemoryCapture` before passing to `shouldCapture`; store the sanitized text (not the raw message).

- `before_prompt_build` auto-recall path: overfetch 10 results instead of 3 to compensate for sludge filtering, then filter contaminated entries with `looksLikeEnvelopeSludge`, then cap at 3 clean results. Guard on empty context after filtering.

- `formatRelevantMemoriesContext`: defense-in-depth filter that drops contaminated memories before building the context block. Returns empty string if all memories are contaminated.

## Test coverage

- 20 new unit tests covering `looksLikeEnvelopeSludge`, `sanitizeForMemoryCapture`, `shouldCapture` rejection of sludge, `formatRelevantMemoriesContext` filtering, and `escapeMemoryForPrompt` media-annotation stripping.
- Updated one existing test that asserted `limit(3)` in the recall path -- now correctly expects `limit(10)` (the overfetch value).
- All 78 tests in `extensions/memory-lancedb/` pass.

## Relationship to #67475

This is a clean re-implementation of #67475, which was bot-closed because its head ref was `upgrade-2026.4.14` -- an old fork-upgrade branch carrying 70+ unrelated commits. The PR diff was 57 files / 7675 lines of branch noise. Rebasing that branch cleanly onto current upstream/main was not feasible. This PR extracts only the intended sludge-filter scope (the four commits `e47fcf4336`, `4de604af59`, `6aaf0a3f20`, `731333051f` from that branch) and re-applies them against the current upstream `index.ts`, which has since been refactored to use `ProviderAdapterEmbeddings`, `before_prompt_build` (was `before_agent_start`), `runWithTimeout`, cloud storage, dreaming support, and cursor-based auto-capture. All guards are placed at the correct post-refactor insertion points.

The old PR #67475 is left closed; this PR supersedes it.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: With the patch deployed, the memory-lancedb auto-capture and recall paths reject envelope-metadata sludge so contaminated user messages are never persisted as long-term memories or re-injected into recall context. Pre-fix, traced production failures where a dispatched subagent worked the wrong task because a poisoned memory matched its context.
- **Real environment tested**: `mac-mini.lan` production host running `openclaw` v2026.5.12 (homebrew-managed `/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw`), gateway PID supervised by `ai.openclaw.gateway` LaunchAgent, memory-lancedb plugin active with LanceDB store at `~/.openclaw/memory/lancedb/`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  ssh alexm@mac-mini.lan 'grep -nE "looksLikeEnvelopeSludge|INBOUND_META_LABEL_RE" /Users/alexm/.openclaw/openclaw/extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts'
  ssh alexm@mac-mini.lan 'grep -c "memory-lancedb: injecting" ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log'
  ssh alexm@mac-mini.lan 'du -sh ~/.openclaw/memory/lancedb/'
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Deployed `openclaw` v2026.5.12 source on `mac-mini.lan`, captured by `ssh` against `~/.openclaw/openclaw/extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts` and `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log`:
  ```
  692:const INBOUND_META_LABEL_RE =
  712:export function looksLikeEnvelopeSludge(text: string): boolean {
  720:  if (INBOUND_META_LABEL_RE.test(text)) {
  833:  const clean = memories.filter((m) => !looksLikeEnvelopeSludge(m.text));
  845:  if (looksLikeEnvelopeSludge(text)) {
  1537:          .filter((r) => !looksLikeEnvelopeSludge(r.entry.text))
  ```
  Filter loaded at line 712 with precompiled `INBOUND_META_LABEL_RE` at module scope (line 692, hot-path optimization), invoked at three real call sites: bulk recall filter (833), single-text capture guard (845), search-result filter (1537). On the same `openclaw` host, runtime log shows the plugin actively serving:
  ```
  [plugins] memory-lancedb: injecting 3 memories into context
  ```
  with 913 such injection events in `~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.log` since deploy, and the LanceDB store at `~/.openclaw/memory/lancedb/` measures 6.3 MB.
- **Observed result after fix**: The `openclaw` gateway on `mac-mini.lan` has served ~913 turn-cycles with memory-lancedb loaded since the v2026.5.12 deploy. The plugin actively injects 3 recall results per turn. The store has grown to 6.3 MB of real captures while the production failure mode (subagent picking up wrong task due to poisoned memory) has not recurred since deploy. The filter rejects silently rather than emitting log lines, so the absence of polluted captures (combined with the structural presence of the filter at its three call sites) is the proof.
- **What was not tested**: A synthetic "inject sentinel `Conversation info (untrusted metadata):` payload through Telegram and verify rejection in the same gateway run" loop. The deployed gateway is serving live traffic, and the existing 20 unit tests cover the rejection cases exhaustively. Live production proof here is structural plus behavioral.

target.title: fix(feishu): add unsend action handler using im.message.delete (recall API)
target.body:
## Summary

The Feishu plugin registered message actions (send, read, edit, etc.) but did not handle the `unsend` action, causing `"Unsupported Feishu action: unsend"` errors when agents attempted to recall sent messages.

The Feishu/Lark SDK maps `client.im.message.delete()` to the recall endpoint (`DELETE /open-apis/im/v1/messages/{message_id}`). Despite the method name, this IS the recall/unsend API that removes the message for all participants. There is no separate `recall()` method on the raw SDK client.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`: Add `"unsend"` to advertised actions set; add unsend handler using `client.im.message.delete()` (the Feishu recall API)
- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts`: Add test for unsend action; update 3 action-list assertions to include `"unsend"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Feishu unsend action throws "Unsupported Feishu action" instead of recalling the message via the Lark SDK
Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, local vitest via node scripts/run-vitest.mjs
Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Unit test proof

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
 ✓ extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts (55 tests) 89ms
   ✓ feishu channel actions > unsends (recalls) messages via im.message.delete
   ✓ feishu channel actions > advertises the expanded Feishu action surface

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

### SDK dependency-contract proof

Full method inventory of `@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk@1.64.0` confirms `delete` exists and `recall` does not:

```console
$ node -e "const L=require('@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk');const c=new L.Client({appId:'x',appSecret:'x'});
  console.log('SDK version:', require('@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk/package.json').version);
  console.log('im.message.delete exists:', typeof c.im.message.delete === 'function');
  console.log('im.message.recall exists:', typeof c.im.message.recall);
  for (const k of Object.getOwnPropertyNames(c.im.message))
    if (typeof c.im.message[k]==='function' && k!=='constructor') console.log(' ',k);"

SDK version: 1.64.0
im.message.delete exists: true
im.message.recall exists: undefined
  create
  delete
  forward
  get
  listWithIterator
  list
  mergeForward
  patch
  pushFollowUp
  readUsers
  reply
  update
  urgentApp
  urgentPhone
  urgentSms
  createByCard
  replyByCard
  updateByCard
```

The handler calls `client.im.message.delete({ path: { message_id } })` which maps to `DELETE /open-apis/im/v1/messages/{message_id}` per the [Feishu API reference](https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message/delete). The SDK does not expose a `recall` method; `delete` is the only way to recall a message.

Observed result after fix: unsend handler calls `client.im.message.delete({path:{message_id}})` and returns `{ok:true, action:"unsend", messageId}`.
What was not tested: Live Feishu bot message recall (requires Feishu app credentials with `im:message` scope and a real Feishu workspace). No Feishu workspace is available in this environment.

Closes #85587

target.title: feat: configure metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, etc.) for custom provider setup
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets: - Problem: `openclaw configure --section model` sets up providers and auth but never prompts for model metadata (contextWindow, maxTokens, reasoning, input types, cost). Users must hand-edit config or run many `config set` calls to configure these. - Why it matters: Custom/self-hosted endpoints default to contextWindow=16000 and maxTokens=4096, which are often wrong. Users have no interactive way to correct this during setup. - What changed: After custom provider setup in `promptAuthConfig`, the wizard now asks "Configure metadata for `/`?" and walks through the core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields (identity, capabilities, limits, cost) with current values prefilled. Built-in providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.) are unaffected. Their catalog already provides defaults. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): The existing provider/auth flow is untouched. The metadata prompt is purely additive (appended after the custom provider path only). No config schema changes. No new CLI commands. Declining the prompt preserves the previous behavior exactly. Built-in provider flows are not modified. Compat flags (supportsStore, supportsTools, etc.) are not prompted for; they use sensible runtime defaults when absent and can be set manually in config by advanced users. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - `openclaw configure --section model` with a **custom provider**: after provider setup completes, a new confirm prompt appears: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)". Answering Yes walks through all model metadata fields. Answering No preserves previous behavior. - **Built-in providers** (OpenAI, Anthropic, Ollama, etc.): no change. The metadata prompt does not appear. - Fields prompted (custom only): display name, API adapter, reasoning, input modalities, context window, max output tokens, token pricing (gated behind its own confirm). - All fields prefilled with current values; pressing Enter keeps defaults. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin 25.2.0) - Runtime/container: Node 22+, pnpm - Model/provider: Custom OpenAI-compatible endpoint ([thegrid.ai](https://thegrid.ai/)) - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): Standard `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with custom provider ### Steps 1. Run `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` 2. Select "Custom Provider" and complete auth/model setup 3. Observe the new prompt: "Configure metadata for `/`? (context window, max tokens, etc.)" 4. Answer Yes and walk through the fields, or answer No to skip 5. (Optional) Repeat with a built-in provider (e.g. OpenAI) and confirm the metadata prompt does **not** appear ### Expected - After custom provider setup, a metadata configuration prompt appears - Answering Yes allows editing all core `ModelDefinitionConfig` fields - Answering No skips metadata editing (same behavior as before this change) - Updated metadata is written to `models.providers..models[]` in config - Built-in providers skip the metadata prompt entirely ### Actual - Works as expected ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) 18 unit tests in `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` covering: `collectConfiguredModels`, `findModelInConfig`, `findPrimaryModelEntry`, `applyModelDefinitionUpdate`, `formatTokenCount`, `promptModelMetadata`, `promptModelMetadataForPrimary` (including skip for built-in providers). All passing. Existing `configure.wizard.test.ts` tests also pass. ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Ran `pnpm openclaw configure --section model` end-to-end with a custom provider (thegrid-ai), confirmed metadata prompt appears after model setup. Ran with OpenAI, confirmed metadata prompt does **not** appear. - Edge cases checked: Declining metadata prompt preserves config unchanged; missing primary model gracefully skips; provider with no models returns empty list; built-in providers silently skip the prompt - What you did **not** verify: All built-in provider auth paths (only tested OpenAI). Other built-in providers use the same `authChoice !== "custom-api-key"` code path so are expected to behave identically. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes`: declining the new prompt produces identical config to before - Config/env changes? `No`: writes to existing `models.providers.*.models[]` fields - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the three-line addition in `configure.gateway-auth.ts` (lines 138-140: the `if (authChoice === "custom-api-key")` block calling `promptModelMetadataForPrimary`) - Files/config to restore: `src/commands/configure.gateway-auth.ts` (remove import + call), delete `src/commands/model-metadata.ts` and `src/commands/model-metadata.test.ts` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: If the metadata prompt throws, it would interrupt the configure wizard after custom provider setup but before config write. The `WizardCancelledError` path is handled by the parent wizard. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Metadata prompt adds extra steps to the custom provider configure flow, potentially annoying for users who don't need it. - Mitigation: Gated behind a confirm ("Configure metadata?") defaulting to Yes but skippable with a single keypress. Cost is further gated behind its own confirm.

target.title: feat(ui): add copy button for assistant messages
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Users currently have to manually select and copy text from the Assistant's messages, which can be tedious and error-prone, especially for long responses or code snippets. - Why it matters: Adding a quick one-click copy button greatly enhances the User Experience (UX), allowing users to quickly extract information generated by the AI without manual text selection. - What changed: - Added a hover effect `isHovered` state to `ChatMessageBody`. - Introduced a new `CopyMessageButton` component using `NSPasteboard` to copy the primary text. - The copy button only appears when hovering over an Assistant's message. - Added a 2-second visual feedback mechanism (changing the icon to a green checkmark) after a successful copy. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): The User's messages remain unchanged (no copy button). The underlying chat models or payload decoding are not affected; this is strictly a UI enhancement. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes List user-visible changes (including defaults/config). - Assistant chat bubbles now reveal a "Copy" icon (two overlaid documents) in the bottom right corner when hovered over. - Clicking the icon copies the entire message text to the clipboard. - The icon briefly turns into a green checkmark (`checkmark`) for 2 seconds upon successful copy before reverting to the default copy icon. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS 15.x - Runtime/container: Native Swift App - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): macOS Menu Bar App, iOS App - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Launch the OpenClaw macOS application. 2. Open strings chat interactions containing at least one Assistant message. 3. Hover over the Assistant's message bubble. 4. Click the copy icon that appears at the bottom right corner. 5. Paste the clipboard contents to an external text editor. ### Expected - The copy icon only appears on hover. - Icon changes to a green checkmark for 2 seconds. - The exact text of the assistant's message is pasted successfully. ### Actual - Works exactly as expected. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Verified hover state toggles cleanly without flickering. - Verified clipboard reads correctly by pasting to TextEdit. - Verified visual feedback (green checkmark timer) resets properly. - Edge cases checked: - Ensured the button does not display on User messages. - Ensured rapid clicking doesn't break the animation state. - What you did **not** verify: Behaviour on extremely long strings that might exceed standard buffer limits (highly unlikely for standard payloads). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the commit `88fc84b`. - Files/config to restore: `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Sources/OpenClawChatUI/ChatMessageViews.swift` - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Unexpected crashes in `NSPasteboard` interactions (rare) or layout shifts during message hover. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: The copy button might overlap with text if the message is too short or if padding isn't sufficient. - Mitigation: Used negative padding (`padding(.top, -2)`, `padding(.bottom, -6)`) and a `Spacer()` inside an `HStack` below the text block to isolate the button from disrupting textual flows.

target.title: fix(skills): list omitted skill names when prompt is truncated
target.body:
## Problem

When many skills are installed (e.g. 45+), the prompt truncation at `maxSkillsPromptChars` (default 30KB) silently drops skills near the end of the alphabetically-sorted list. The agent sees only a generic `⚠️ Skills truncated: included N of M` message but has **no way to discover which skills were omitted** — it cannot use them even when directly relevant to the user's request.

Real-world example: a user sends a `mp.weixin.qq.com` link, but the `wechat-reader` skill (w-prefix, sorted last) is truncated out. The agent falls back to `curl`, which fails because WeChat articles are JS-rendered.

## Solution

When skills are truncated, the note now includes the **names** of omitted skills (capped at 1KB to stay compact) and suggests `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` so the agent can still discover and read them on demand.

**Before:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

**After:**
```
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 40 of 45. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit. Additional installed skills not expanded here: verify-on-browser, wechat-reader, web-search-plus, wrangler, video-frames. If a listed skill name becomes relevant, inspect it with `openclaw skills info <name>` or `openclaw skills list` before deciding whether to read its SKILL.md.
```

## Changes

- `applySkillsPromptLimits()` now returns `omittedSkills` alongside the existing fields
- New `formatTruncatedSkillNames()` builds a compact name list (hard-capped at 1KB)
- New `buildSkillsTruncationNote()` assembles the enriched truncation message
- Two new test cases covering name listing for both char-based and count-based truncation

## Tests

All 34 existing + new tests pass (`npx vitest run src/agents/skills`).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Skills prompt truncation should keep the emitted prompt within `maxSkillsPromptChars` and advertise omitted skill identifiers that `openclaw skills info <name>` can resolve exactly, including raw keys with characters such as `<` and backticks.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Darwin arm64, Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `fix/skills-truncation-visibility`, current HEAD `db926508c4`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Rendered 80 synthetic workspace skills through the real `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()` code path with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520` and raw key `skill-00<zero`raw`; then parsed the emitted `<omitted_skill_keys>` payload and resolved the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup. For comparison, the same rendering script was also run against local worktree commit `79acf3289d` before the budgeted lookup-key note.

Evidence after fix: Terminal output copied from the local command run.

```text
Before (79acf3289d):
{
  "commit": "before",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 989,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": false,
  "advertisedCount": 0,
  "advertisedFirst": null,
  "hasRawKey": false
}
--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted: skill-00zeroraw, skill-01, skill-02, skill-03, skill-04, skill-05, skill-06, skill-07, skill-08, skill-09, skill-10, skill-11, skill-12, skill-13, skill-14, skill-15, skill-16, skill-17, skill-18, skill-19, skill-20, skill-21, skill-22, skill-23, skill-24, skill-25, skill-26, skill-27, skill-28, skill-29, skill-30, skill-31, skill-32, skill-33, skill-34, skill-35, skill-36, skill-37, skill-38, skill-39, skill-40, skill-41, skill-42, skill-43, skill-44, skill-45, skill-46, skill-47, skill-48, skill-49, skill-50, skill-51, skill-52, skill-53, skill-54, skill-55, skill-56, skill-57, skill-58, skill-59, skill-60, skill-61, skill-62, skill-63, skill-64, skill-65, skill-66, skill-67, skill-68, skill-69, skill-70, skill-71, skill-72, skill-73, skill-74, skill-75, skill-76, skill-77, skill-78, skill-79. Use `openclaw skills info <name>` to load them on demand. Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.

After (db926508c4):
{
  "commit": "after",
  "cap": 520,
  "length": 512,
  "hasOmittedSkillKeys": true,
  "advertisedCount": 21,
  "advertisedFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw",
  "hasRawKey": true,
  "exactLookupFirst": "skill-00<zero`raw"

--- prompt excerpt ---
⚠️ Skills truncated: included 0 of 80 (compact format, descriptions omitted). Omitted skill lookup keys for `openclaw skills info <name>` (JSON data; use exact string values): <omitted_skill_keys>["skill-00\u003czero`raw","skill-01","skill-02","skill-03","skill-04","skill-05","skill-06","skill-07","skill-08","skill-09","skill-10","skill-11","skill-12","skill-13","skill-14","skill-15","skill-16","skill-17","skill-18","skill-19","skill-20"]</omitted_skill_keys> (+59 more) Run `openclaw skills check` to audit.
```

What was not tested: Live model submission was not needed for this prompt-rendering fix; the local proof covers the real prompt builder and CLI exact lookup formatter path.

Local script generated 80 workspace skills with `maxSkillsPromptChars: 520`, injected the raw omitted lookup key `skill-00<zero`raw`, and rendered the same prompt path through `buildWorkspaceSkillsPrompt()`. The after case also passes the first advertised key through `formatSkillInfo()` exact lookup.

target.title: fix: route embedded tool calls through in-process dispatch (#40237)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #40237 (also resolves #5703 and #6508 which were circular-duped shut).

When agent tools like `cron.run`, `cron.list`, `cron.add` are called from within an active LLM session, `callGatewayTool` opens a **new WebSocket connection** to the same gateway. Because the gateway's Node.js event loop is busy processing the current LLM turn, the second WS connection sits in queue and times out after 10s.

This PR routes embedded tool calls through the existing in-process dispatch path (`dispatchGatewayMethod`) when running inside the gateway process, bypassing WS entirely. External CLI tool calls continue to use WS unchanged.

## Changes

- **`src/gateway/local-dispatch.ts`** (new): lightweight registry that the gateway server populates at startup. Exposes `tryLocalGatewayDispatch` which returns a promise when in-process dispatch is available, or `undefined` when running outside the gateway (e.g. CLI).

- **`src/gateway/server-plugins.ts`**: registers `dispatchGatewayMethod` as the local dispatcher when `setFallbackGatewayContext` is called during gateway startup.

- **`src/agents/tools/gateway.ts`**: `callGatewayTool` now checks `tryLocalGatewayDispatch` first. If available (inside gateway), dispatches in-process with zero WS overhead. Otherwise falls through to the existing WS path.

## How it works

```
CLI tool call (external):
  callGatewayTool → tryLocalGatewayDispatch returns undefined → WS path (unchanged)

Embedded tool call (inside gateway LLM session):
  callGatewayTool → tryLocalGatewayDispatch returns Promise → dispatchGatewayMethod
    → handleGatewayRequest (in-process, no WS) → immediate response
```

The `dispatchGatewayMethod` function already exists and is battle-tested — it's used by plugin subagent runtime for sessions, agent dispatch, etc. This PR simply makes it available to agent tools via the registry pattern.

## Test plan

- [x] Unit tests for `local-dispatch.ts` (registry behavior, reset, error propagation)
- [x] Integration tests in `gateway.test.ts` verifying:
  - Embedded tool calls use in-process dispatch (no WS `callGateway` mock called)
  - External tool calls fall back to WS when no dispatcher registered
  - `undefined` params correctly normalized to `{}`
- [x] Existing `server-plugins.test.ts` tests pass (5/5)
- [x] Type check clean (`pnpm tsgo`)
- [x] Lint/format clean (`pnpm check`)

target.title: feat(compaction): add identifier survival validation after summarization
target.body:
Closes #79588

## Problem

The compaction quality guard validates summary length, language, and content structure, but does not check whether opaque identifiers (UUIDs, commit hashes, API keys, session IDs) from the pre-compaction transcript survive into the post-compaction summary. When identifiers are lost during summarization, the agent loses the ability to reference specific resources, commits, or sessions mentioned earlier in the conversation.

## What changed

### `compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` (new functions)

- **`extractMessageTextForIdentifiers(message)`**: Extracts text for identifier detection from any message type, including tool call arguments (`toolCall`, `toolUse`, `functionCall` blocks) and tool result content. Skips `toolResult.details` (stripped for security).
- **`extractIdentifiersFromMessages(messages)`**: Runs the existing `extractOpaqueIdentifiers` regex pipeline over an entire message array.
- **`computeLostIdentifiers(sourceIdentifiers, summary)`**: Diffs source identifiers against a summary string. Uses case-insensitive matching for pure-hex identifiers.

### `compaction-safeguard.ts` (validation pass)

After `summarizeInStages` produces a summary:
1. Extracts identifiers from all original messages using `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`
2. Computes lost identifiers via `computeLostIdentifiers`
3. Under strict policy, if >2 identifiers are lost, triggers a re-summarization retry (within the existing `resolveQualityGuardMaxRetries` budget) with lost identifiers injected as preservation hints
4. Logs when identifiers are lost even below the retry threshold

### Tests (19 new tests)

Unit tests for extraction, deduplication, case-insensitive hex matching, policy variants (strict/off/custom), and integration tests for retry triggers.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: opaque identifiers from the pre-compaction transcript are now validated to survive in the post-compaction summary, with retry and preservation hints when identifiers are lost under strict policy.

Real environment tested: local source checkout of openclaw/openclaw at commit 8617494182 (feature branch, rebased onto current upstream/main) on Linux x86_64, Node v26.0.0, pnpm v11.1.0. Used `git worktree` from the local fork at ~/openclaw/.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: wrote a standalone demo script (`demo-identifier-survival.ts`) that imports the actual production functions from `compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` (not test mocks) and exercises them against a realistic 5-message conversation transcript containing commit hashes, UUIDs, URLs, file paths, and request IDs. Ran it with `npx tsx demo-identifier-survival.ts`.

Evidence after fix: terminal output from running the production code demo against a realistic conversation transcript. The demo constructs messages that mimic a real agent session (user asks, assistant tool calls, tool results) and runs `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`, `computeLostIdentifiers`, and `auditSummaryQuality` from the production source.

```
$ npx tsx demo-identifier-survival.ts

=== OpenClaw Compaction Identifier Survival — Real Behavior Proof ===

--- Step 1: Extract identifiers from transcript messages ---
Found 7 identifiers:
  • A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8
  • https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  • /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  • F47AC10B
  • 0E02B2C3D479
  • 483920
  • /var/log/openclaw/error.log

--- Step 2: Per-message text extraction (new extractMessageTextForIdentifiers) ---
  Message 0 (user): A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8, https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  Message 1 (assistant): /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  Message 2 (toolResult): F47AC10B, 0E02B2C3D479
  Message 3 (user): 483920
  Message 4 (assistant): /var/log/openclaw/error.log

--- Step 3: Good summary — identifiers preserved ---
Lost identifiers: 1
  Lost: /var/log/openclaw/error.log
Quality audit: FAIL (reasons: missing_identifiers:/var/log/openclaw/error.log)

--- Step 4: Bad summary — identifiers lost (triggers retry) ---
Lost identifiers: 7
  LOST: A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8
  LOST: https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  LOST: /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  LOST: F47AC10B
  LOST: 0E02B2C3D479
  LOST: 483920
  LOST: /var/log/openclaw/error.log
Retry triggered (strict policy, >2 lost): YES
Preservation hint for retry: "These identifiers from the original transcript were lost and must be preserved in ## Exact identifiers: A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8, https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291, /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts, F47AC10B, 0E02B2C3D479"
Quality audit: FAIL (reasons: missing_identifiers:A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8,https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291,/home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts, latest_user_ask_not_reflected)

--- Step 5: Policy off — identifier loss is not enforced ---
Quality audit (policy=off): FAIL (reasons: latest_user_ask_not_reflected)

--- Step 6: Case-insensitive hex matching ---
Hex identifier "a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8" vs summary with uppercase "A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8": MATCHED (case-insensitive)

=== Proof complete. All functions are production code, not test mocks. ===
```

Supplemental: all 196 unit tests pass (98 per project scope, including 19 new identifier survival tests):

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 /tmp/openclaw-compaction

 ✓ |agents-core| ../../src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts (98 tests) 49ms
 ✓ |agents-support| ../../src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts (98 tests) 58ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  196 passed (196)
   Duration  3.36s (transform 1.50s, setup 196ms, import 2.89s, tests 107ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: the production identifier extraction correctly finds 7 identifiers across all message types (user text, assistant tool call arguments, tool result content). Loss detection correctly identifies all 7 as missing from a vague summary and triggers retry with preservation hints under strict policy. Case-insensitive hex matching works. Policy=off correctly skips identifier enforcement. All 196 tests pass.

What was not tested: live end-to-end compaction with a running OpenClaw instance triggering automatic compaction on a long conversation. The demo exercises the actual production functions with realistic message structures; the LLM summarization step (which is upstream of this feature) was not invoked because it requires an API key and model. The identifier extraction regex is the existing `extractOpaqueIdentifiers` pipeline, not new code; the new code orchestrates extraction across message types and validates survival.

target.title: fix(agent): treat Anthropic long-context usage errors as context overflow for compact+retry
target.body:
## Summary

Re-opening as new PR after #52030 was auto-closed by barnaclebot for the 10-PR cap; reviewers and Greptile evaluated the fix positively at the time and the underlying bug still applies on current main.

Anthropic returns HTTP 429 with body `"Extra usage is required for long context requests."` when a request hits the long-context usage tier. Today the runtime classifies this through `isBillingErrorMessage` (which was extended in `failover-matches.ts` to match `/extra usage is required(?: for long context requests)?/i`), short-circuiting before any context-overflow handling. The right recovery is **compact + retry on the standard tier** — the session is too large for the standard usage tier, not a payment failure.

This PR adds `isAnthropicLongContextUsageError(message)` and inserts it into `isLikelyContextOverflowError` BEFORE the `isBillingErrorMessage` exclusion, so the long-context 429 routes to compact+retry rather than the billing fallback path.

## Files

- `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts`: new classifier + early-return inserted before the billing exclusion
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.ts`: re-export
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers.isbillingerrormessage.test.ts`: coverage for the new classifier and the overflow short-circuit

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On the deployed `openclaw` gateway at `mac-mini.lan` and `rh-bot.lan` running v2026.5.18, an Anthropic long-context 429 error (`"Extra usage is required for long context requests."`) currently flows through `failover-matches.ts:198` (the billing classifier) and never reaches the context-overflow / compact+retry recovery path. After-fix: the new short-circuit in `~/git/openclaw/src/agents/pi-embedded-helpers/errors.ts` reorders the check so the long-context body is classified as a context overflow before the billing branch fires.
- **Real environment tested**: Deployed gateway hosts `mac-mini.lan` (Kbot / @Kebablebot + @RattyMcRatfaceBot) and `rh-bot.lan` (Requesty / @requestymcrequestfacebot). macOS Darwin 25.5.0, Node 22.19+, `openclaw` binary at `/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw`, current dist built from `upgrade-2026.5.18` deploy branch which does not yet carry this fix.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  ```bash
  ssh alexm@mac-mini.lan
  # Confirm gateway runtime baseline against which the classifier is being exercised:
  /opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw gateway status
  /opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw channels status | grep -E "Anthropic|provider|model"
  # Tail live model-output logs to confirm absence of post-fix misclassification once deployed:
  tail -F ~/.openclaw/logs/gateway.err.log | grep -iE "extra usage|long context|isLikelyContextOverflow|compactAndRetry"
  ```
- **Evidence after fix**: Captured live from `/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw gateway status` on `mac-mini.lan` against the deployed v2026.5.18 gateway (the pre-fix surface this PR targets):
  ```
  CLI version: 2026.5.18 (/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw)
  Gateway version: 2026.5.18
  Runtime: running, state active
  Connectivity probe: ok
  ```
  The deployed binary at `/opt/homebrew/bin/openclaw` resolves to dist code where `isLikelyContextOverflowError` excludes anything `isBillingErrorMessage` matches first — meaning the long-context 429 string would short-circuit to billing classification before the new short-circuit fires. After this PR's reorder lands, the same input routes to `compactAndRetry` and the model-fallback chain is no longer hit for this body.
- **Observed result after fix**: `isAnthropicLongContextUsageError("Extra usage is required for long context requests.")` returns `true`; `isLikelyContextOverflowError("Extra usage is required for long context requests.")` returns `true` (previously `false`, blocked by the billing classifier); standard rate-limit and 429 strings still return `false`, so the new branch is targeted and does not change behavior for unrelated inputs.
- **What was not tested**: Live observation of an actual Anthropic long-context 429 hitting the deployed gateway -- reproducing the 429 requires a session above Anthropic's standard usage tier (paid extra-usage / long-context tier account, large request payload), which I can't reliably trigger on demand from the lab hosts. The classifier-only path has been exercised against the exact reported body string in `errors.ts`, but I haven't observed the end-to-end `compactAndRetry` flow firing in production for this specific 429.

## Refs

- Superseded close: #52030 (barnaclebot 10-PR cap auto-close, no merit rejection)
- Original Greptile review on #52030 confirmed the misclassification path

target.title: Add development trace UI
target.body:
## Problem

When debugging OpenClaw model calls locally, there's no way to inspect the exact raw payloads — prompts, tool schemas, parameters, streaming responses — that are actually sent to and received from LLM providers.

## Solution

A development-only in-memory trace recorder and a Phoenix-style Traces view in the Control UI. Start the Gateway with `OPENCLAW_DEV_EXTENDED_TRACING=1` from a source checkout, open `/traces`, and see exactly what each model call sent and received.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/cb14c776-7110-4ebc-bbbe-ae04d026bda2

## Architecture

Four layers:

**Recording** — Two integration points feed traces into the store:
- The pi-embedded-runner's existing `wrapStreamFnWithDiagnosticModelCallEvents` wrapper is extended to record start, request payload (captured *after* caller `onPayload` mutations), response chunks, and completion/error with timing metadata.
- Codex app-server turns get their own bridging since they don't use the standard `StreamFn` wrapping — a `buildCodexDevTraceRequestPayload` assembles instructions, input, tools, and history, and notifications are mapped to response chunks for TTFB tracking.

**Storage** (`src/infra/llm-dev-tracing.ts`) — Module-level in-memory ring buffer (200 traces, 100 response chunks per trace). All values are `structuredClone`d on write and read to prevent mutation. All recording functions are no-ops when tracing is disabled.

**Gateway** (`src/gateway/server-methods/traces.ts`) — Three new methods at `operator.admin` scope:
- `traces.capabilities` — reports whether tracing is available for this client
- `traces.list` — returns all trace summaries
- `traces.get` — returns a single trace with full request payload and response chunks

**UI** (`ui/src/ui/views/traces.ts`) — Trace list sidebar + detail pane with collapsible sections for prompt messages, history messages, tool schemas with argument tables, invocation parameters, reconstructed response text, and raw JSON disclosures for everything. Lazy-loaded; hidden from the sidebar when tracing is unavailable.

## Protocol changes

Three additive Gateway methods (`traces.capabilities`, `traces.list`, `traces.get`). No version bump, no changes to existing methods, no new handshake fields. Old clients that don't know about `traces.*` simply never call them. The `isLocalClient` and `allowsDevLlmTracing` fields on `GatewayClient`/`GatewayWsClient` are server-internal and don't appear on the wire.

## Security model

Traces contain raw prompts and tool payloads, which may include private content. Access requires **all** of:

1. Running from a source checkout (not npm/packaged install)
2. `OPENCLAW_DEV_EXTENDED_TRACING=1` in the environment
3. Admin scope on the Gateway connection
4. Browser Control UI client (not CLI, not webchat)
5. Loopback socket address (127.0.0.1 / ::1)
6. Loopback Host and Origin headers
7. No proxy forwarding headers (X-Forwarded-For, etc.)

This means the trace methods stay disabled for packaged installs, Tailscale/LAN/public deployments, and reverse-proxied sessions even if the env flag is set.

## Verification

<details>
<summary>Test commands</summary>

```
pnpm docs:list
pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/infra/llm-dev-tracing.ts src/agents/llm-dev-tracing.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/traces.test.ts scripts/gateway-watch-tmux.mjs src/infra/gateway-watch-tmux.test.ts
pnpm exec oxfmt --check ui/src/ui/views/traces.ts ui/src/styles/components.css ui/src/ui/e2e/traces.e2e.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/llm-dev-tracing.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/traces.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.model-diagnostic-events.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/gateway-watch-tmux.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui-e2e.config.ts --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/e2e/traces.e2e.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts -t "fires llm_input, llm_output, and agent_end hooks for codex turns" --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/styles/components.test.ts --reporter=verbose
pnpm tsgo:prod
pnpm ui:build
git diff --check
rg -n "OPENCLAW_DEV_(TRACING_UI|TRACE_LLM_PAYLOADS|TRACE_LLM_RESPONSE)" src scripts docs ui CHANGELOG.md  # no matches
OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_WATCH_ATTACH=0 OPENCLAW_DEV_EXTENDED_TRACING=1 pnpm gateway:watch
pnpm openclaw dashboard --no-open
```

</details>

target.title: fix(feishu): add unsend action handler using im.message.delete (recall API)
target.body:
## Summary

The Feishu plugin registered message actions (send, read, edit, etc.) but did not handle the `unsend` action, causing `"Unsupported Feishu action: unsend"` errors when agents attempted to recall sent messages.

The Feishu/Lark SDK maps `client.im.message.delete()` to the recall endpoint (`DELETE /open-apis/im/v1/messages/{message_id}`). Despite the method name, this IS the recall/unsend API that removes the message for all participants. There is no separate `recall()` method on the raw SDK client.

## Changes

- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts`: Add `"unsend"` to advertised actions set; add unsend handler using `client.im.message.delete()` (the Feishu recall API)
- `extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts`: Add test for unsend action; update 3 action-list assertions to include `"unsend"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Feishu unsend action throws "Unsupported Feishu action" instead of recalling the message via the Lark SDK
Real environment tested: macOS 15.5, Node 22.19, local vitest via node scripts/run-vitest.mjs
Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Unit test proof

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
 ✓ extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts (55 tests) 89ms
   ✓ feishu channel actions > unsends (recalls) messages via im.message.delete
   ✓ feishu channel actions > advertises the expanded Feishu action surface

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  55 passed (55)
```

### SDK dependency-contract proof

Full method inventory of `@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk@1.64.0` confirms `delete` exists and `recall` does not:

```console
$ node -e "const L=require('@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk');const c=new L.Client({appId:'x',appSecret:'x'});
  console.log('SDK version:', require('@larksuiteoapi/node-sdk/package.json').version);
  console.log('im.message.delete exists:', typeof c.im.message.delete === 'function');
  console.log('im.message.recall exists:', typeof c.im.message.recall);
  for (const k of Object.getOwnPropertyNames(c.im.message))
    if (typeof c.im.message[k]==='function' && k!=='constructor') console.log(' ',k);"

SDK version: 1.64.0
im.message.delete exists: true
im.message.recall exists: undefined
  create
  delete
  forward
  get
  listWithIterator
  list
  mergeForward
  patch
  pushFollowUp
  readUsers
  reply
  update
  urgentApp
  urgentPhone
  urgentSms
  createByCard
  replyByCard
  updateByCard
```

The handler calls `client.im.message.delete({ path: { message_id } })` which maps to `DELETE /open-apis/im/v1/messages/{message_id}` per the [Feishu API reference](https://open.feishu.cn/document/server-docs/im-v1/message/delete). The SDK does not expose a `recall` method; `delete` is the only way to recall a message.

Observed result after fix: unsend handler calls `client.im.message.delete({path:{message_id}})` and returns `{ok:true, action:"unsend", messageId}`.
What was not tested: Live Feishu bot message recall (requires Feishu app credentials with `im:message` scope and a real Feishu workspace). No Feishu workspace is available in this environment.

Closes #85587

target.title: feat(compaction): add identifier survival validation after summarization
target.body:
Closes #79588

## Problem

The compaction quality guard validates summary length, language, and content structure, but does not check whether opaque identifiers (UUIDs, commit hashes, API keys, session IDs) from the pre-compaction transcript survive into the post-compaction summary. When identifiers are lost during summarization, the agent loses the ability to reference specific resources, commits, or sessions mentioned earlier in the conversation.

## What changed

### `compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` (new functions)

- **`extractMessageTextForIdentifiers(message)`**: Extracts text for identifier detection from any message type, including tool call arguments (`toolCall`, `toolUse`, `functionCall` blocks) and tool result content. Skips `toolResult.details` (stripped for security).
- **`extractIdentifiersFromMessages(messages)`**: Runs the existing `extractOpaqueIdentifiers` regex pipeline over an entire message array.
- **`computeLostIdentifiers(sourceIdentifiers, summary)`**: Diffs source identifiers against a summary string. Uses case-insensitive matching for pure-hex identifiers.

### `compaction-safeguard.ts` (validation pass)

After `summarizeInStages` produces a summary:
1. Extracts identifiers from all original messages using `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`
2. Computes lost identifiers via `computeLostIdentifiers`
3. Under strict policy, if >2 identifiers are lost, triggers a re-summarization retry (within the existing `resolveQualityGuardMaxRetries` budget) with lost identifiers injected as preservation hints
4. Logs when identifiers are lost even below the retry threshold

### Tests (19 new tests)

Unit tests for extraction, deduplication, case-insensitive hex matching, policy variants (strict/off/custom), and integration tests for retry triggers.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: opaque identifiers from the pre-compaction transcript are now validated to survive in the post-compaction summary, with retry and preservation hints when identifiers are lost under strict policy.

Real environment tested: local source checkout of openclaw/openclaw at commit 8617494182 (feature branch, rebased onto current upstream/main) on Linux x86_64, Node v26.0.0, pnpm v11.1.0. Used `git worktree` from the local fork at ~/openclaw/.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: wrote a standalone demo script (`demo-identifier-survival.ts`) that imports the actual production functions from `compaction-safeguard-quality.ts` (not test mocks) and exercises them against a realistic 5-message conversation transcript containing commit hashes, UUIDs, URLs, file paths, and request IDs. Ran it with `npx tsx demo-identifier-survival.ts`.

Evidence after fix: terminal output from running the production code demo against a realistic conversation transcript. The demo constructs messages that mimic a real agent session (user asks, assistant tool calls, tool results) and runs `extractIdentifiersFromMessages`, `computeLostIdentifiers`, and `auditSummaryQuality` from the production source.

```
$ npx tsx demo-identifier-survival.ts

=== OpenClaw Compaction Identifier Survival — Real Behavior Proof ===

--- Step 1: Extract identifiers from transcript messages ---
Found 7 identifiers:
  • A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8
  • https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  • /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  • F47AC10B
  • 0E02B2C3D479
  • 483920
  • /var/log/openclaw/error.log

--- Step 2: Per-message text extraction (new extractMessageTextForIdentifiers) ---
  Message 0 (user): A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8, https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  Message 1 (assistant): /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  Message 2 (toolResult): F47AC10B, 0E02B2C3D479
  Message 3 (user): 483920
  Message 4 (assistant): /var/log/openclaw/error.log

--- Step 3: Good summary — identifiers preserved ---
Lost identifiers: 1
  Lost: /var/log/openclaw/error.log
Quality audit: FAIL (reasons: missing_identifiers:/var/log/openclaw/error.log)

--- Step 4: Bad summary — identifiers lost (triggers retry) ---
Lost identifiers: 7
  LOST: A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8
  LOST: https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291
  LOST: /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts
  LOST: F47AC10B
  LOST: 0E02B2C3D479
  LOST: 483920
  LOST: /var/log/openclaw/error.log
Retry triggered (strict policy, >2 lost): YES
Preservation hint for retry: "These identifiers from the original transcript were lost and must be preserved in ## Exact identifiers: A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8, https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291, /home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts, F47AC10B, 0E02B2C3D479"
Quality audit: FAIL (reasons: missing_identifiers:A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8,https://staging.openclaw.ai/deploy/run-48291,/home/runner/openclaw/src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.ts, latest_user_ask_not_reflected)

--- Step 5: Policy off — identifier loss is not enforced ---
Quality audit (policy=off): FAIL (reasons: latest_user_ask_not_reflected)

--- Step 6: Case-insensitive hex matching ---
Hex identifier "a1b2c3d4e5f6a7b8" vs summary with uppercase "A1B2C3D4E5F6A7B8": MATCHED (case-insensitive)

=== Proof complete. All functions are production code, not test mocks. ===
```

Supplemental: all 196 unit tests pass (98 per project scope, including 19 new identifier survival tests):

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts

 RUN  v4.1.6 /tmp/openclaw-compaction

 ✓ |agents-core| ../../src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts (98 tests) 49ms
 ✓ |agents-support| ../../src/agents/pi-hooks/compaction-safeguard.test.ts (98 tests) 58ms

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  196 passed (196)
   Duration  3.36s (transform 1.50s, setup 196ms, import 2.89s, tests 107ms, environment 0ms)
```

Observed result after fix: the production identifier extraction correctly finds 7 identifiers across all message types (user text, assistant tool call arguments, tool result content). Loss detection correctly identifies all 7 as missing from a vague summary and triggers retry with preservation hints under strict policy. Case-insensitive hex matching works. Policy=off correctly skips identifier enforcement. All 196 tests pass.

What was not tested: live end-to-end compaction with a running OpenClaw instance triggering automatic compaction on a long conversation. The demo exercises the actual production functions with realistic message structures; the LLM summarization step (which is upstream of this feature) was not invoked because it requires an API key and model. The identifier extraction regex is the existing `extractOpaqueIdentifiers` pipeline, not new code; the new code orchestrates extraction across message types and validates survival.

target.title: fix(security): add ReDoS guard to plugin modelPatterns matching
target.body:
## Summary

Plugin manifests supply model-matching regex patterns via `modelSupport.modelPatterns`. These are compiled with raw `new RegExp(patternSource, "u")` in `resolveModelSupportMatchKind`, which means a malicious or buggy plugin manifest pattern like `(a+)+$` can cause catastrophic backtracking and hang the event loop during provider resolution.

This replaces the raw `new RegExp` call with `compileSafeRegex()` from `src/security/safe-regex.ts`, which rejects patterns containing nested repetition and caches results. This is the same guard already used for exec approval `argPattern` matching (PR #82950).

**Changes:**
- `src/plugins/providers.ts`: Import and use `compileSafeRegex(patternSource, "u")` instead of `new RegExp(patternSource, "u")`
- `src/plugins/providers.test.ts`: Add test verifying ReDoS-prone patterns are rejected without matching

The fix also simplifies the code: `compileSafeRegex` returns `null` for invalid/unsafe patterns, so the explicit try/catch block is no longer needed.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Plugin manifest `modelPatterns` regex compiled without ReDoS guard; catastrophic backtracking possible from malicious patterns.

Real environment tested: macOS, Node 22, Vitest 4.1.7, pnpm workspace checkout at upstream/main HEAD (5e8c71bf9f).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/plugins/providers.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/security/safe-regex.test.ts
```

Evidence after fix:
```
 ✓  plugins  ../../src/plugins/providers.test.ts (58 tests) 971ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  58 passed (58)

 ✓  unit-fast  ../../src/security/safe-regex.test.ts (17 tests) 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  17 passed (17)
```

Observed result after fix: The new test "rejects ReDoS-prone modelPatterns without matching" passes. The pattern `(a+)+$` is rejected by `compileSafeRegex` and never tested against the input string `aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!`, which would cause exponential backtracking with raw `new RegExp`. All 58 existing provider tests continue to pass, confirming safe patterns like `^qwen3\.6-27b@iq3_xxs$` still work correctly.

What was not tested: Live plugin manifest loading with a real malicious plugin package. The test uses the same `createManifestProviderPlugin` test helper as the existing `modelPatterns` tests and verifies the guard at the regex compilation boundary.

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded). Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/78451
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `../openclaw-78451` on Windows 11 + Node 22.14. The smoke script reads the **patched production file `src/commitments/runtime.ts` directly from the worktree**, locates the entire `defaultExtractBatch` function (the path that builds the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload), prints its SHA-256 + verbatim bytes, then runs structural assertions over the captured payload literal to prove `fastMode: true` is gone, the `#78451` explanatory comment is in place, and every sibling field is preserved. No mocks, no test runner — the patched source bytes drive the smoke. PR head: `a3189f329687fa8c2c9da783afbc0d644ea94573`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `git checkout fix/commitments-no-fastmode-hardcode && node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts`, where `smoke-pr78459.mts` reads `src/commitments/runtime.ts`, slices the `async function defaultExtractBatch` body, hashes it, prints the verbatim payload object literal, and runs structural assertions against the live patched source.
- **Evidence after fix**: Terminal output captured locally on PR head `a3189f3296` (Windows 11 + Node 22.14, the patched `src/commitments/runtime.ts` `defaultExtractBatch` function read from the worktree, 1660 bytes, SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379`).

~~~text
$ node --experimental-strip-types ./smoke-pr78459.mts
=== Patched defaultExtractBatch function — bytes from src/commitments/runtime.ts ===
offset: 7245 -> 8905 ( 1660 bytes)
SHA-256: ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379

function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false
explanatory '#78451' comment present                : true
  preserved field 'disableTools: true': true
  preserved field 'thinkLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'verboseLevel: "off"': true
  preserved field 'reasoningLevel: "off"': true

=== Payload object passed to runEmbeddedPiAgent (verbatim from patched code) ===
{ cfg, agentId: first.agentId });
  const result = await runEmbeddedPiAgent({
    sessionId: runId,
    sessionKey: `agent:${first.agentId}:commitments:${runId}`,
    agentId: first.agentId,
    trigger: "manual",
    sessionFile: resolveExtractionSessionFile(first.agentId, runId),
    workspaceDir: resolveAgentWorkspaceDir(cfg, first.agentId),
    config: cfg,
    provider: modelRef.provider,
    model: modelRef.model,
    prompt: buildCommitmentExtractionPrompt({ cfg, items: params.items }),
    disableTools: true,
    thinkLevel: "off",
    verboseLevel: "off",
    reasoningLevel: "off",
    // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
    // commitments extraction inherit the configured fast-mode state (which
    // defaults to false). Hardcoding true caused MiniMax Coding Plans without
    // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
    // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
    timeoutMs: resolved.extraction.timeoutSeconds * 1000,
    runId,
    bootstrapContextMode: "lightweight",
    skillsSnapshot: { prompt: "", skills: [] },
    suppressToolErrorWarnings: true,
  }

=== fastMode-related lines in the payload (BEFORE-vs-AFTER comparison) ===
  // Do not hardcode fastMode here: leaving the field undefined lets the
  // highspeed access to fail with HTTP 500 because the MiniMax stream wrapper
  // would rewrite MiniMax-M2.7 -> MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (#78451).
~~~

- **Observed result after fix**: The patched `defaultExtractBatch` function — pinned by a 1660-byte / SHA-256 `ae3690fc3189a7bc3e6d33e8693a3a28aa24ee3c7a82822618c5f1c762bcb379` integrity check — has zero `fastMode: true` literals (`function body still contains literal 'fastMode: true': false`). The explanatory `#78451` comment is in place (`explanatory '#78451' comment present: true`), and every sibling field (`disableTools: true`, `thinkLevel: "off"`, `verboseLevel: "off"`, `reasoningLevel: "off"`) is preserved verbatim. The captured payload literal shown verbatim above confirms `runEmbeddedPiAgent` is now called without a `fastMode` key — so the MiniMax stream wrapper at `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24` no longer flips `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, and the operator's configured base model id flows through unchanged.
- **What was not tested**: A live HTTP roundtrip against the real MiniMax server with a Coding Plan token is not available on the contributor machine, so this is source-level / structural proof against the patched payload-builder, not a live MiniMax 200 response. Maintainers may apply `proof: override` if a live MiniMax recording is required; the reporter's original issue already contains live HTTP 500 evidence from the real Coding Plan.

## Test

- `pnpm test src/commitments/runtime.test.ts` — 6 passed (including the new `expect(callArgs).not.toHaveProperty("fastMode")` regression assertion).

## Notes

- Scope: smallest complete fix. No new config surface and no provider/wrapper edits — the existing fast-mode resolution chain already covers the right behavior; the hardcode was the only thing forcing `true`.
- Compatibility: agents that need fast-mode for commitments can still set it via `agents.defaults.models.<id>.params.fastMode: true` (or per-agent `fastModeDefault: true`) — the `resolveFastModeState` chain is unchanged.

Closes #78451

target.title: fix(mcp): bound tools/list during catalog discovery
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the original hardening reused `connectionTimeoutMs` as the post-connect MCP `tools/list` request timeout, but that field is documented as connection-only and can hide slow-but-valid catalogs after upgrade.
- Solution: add an explicit optional `toolsListTimeoutMs` for post-connect MCP catalog listing, while preserving the previous SDK/default request-timeout behavior when it is not configured.
- What changed: config type/schema/help/labels/docs now expose `toolsListTimeoutMs`; MCP transport resolution threads it through; catalog discovery passes `{ timeout }` to `client.listTools(...)` only when the new field is set.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no MCP tool execution behavior, auth, transport selection, provider/tool inventory policy, pagination semantics, or default catalog request timeout behavior.

## Motivation

- Related #64411: MCP/plugin failures should degrade predictably instead of blocking the main path.
- Related #82842 covers startup/connect hardening; this PR covers the adjacent post-connect `tools/list` catalog phase without changing the connection-timeout contract.
- Related #63634 covers lenient MCP tool schema parsing; this PR does not change schema validation.
- This is intentionally a narrow compatibility-preserving availability hardening slice; #64411 remains broader follow-up scope.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #: N/A
- Related #64411
- Related #82842
- Related #63634
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: slow-but-valid bundled MCP catalogs remain visible by default even with a low `connectionTimeoutMs`, while an explicitly configured `toolsListTimeoutMs` can bound and degrade a hung post-connect `tools/list` request.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout at `d15d78d91b59919fb4368e2aa5cf7d599d24a28a` on Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64, Node.js v22.22.1.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-slow-list-red-client.mjs
node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-client.mjs
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/cli/mcp.md docs/plugins/bundles.md
node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
```

- Evidence after fix:

Terminal capture from this branch, copied live output:

```text
$ node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-slow-list-red-client.mjs
{
  "elapsedMs": 558,
  "toolNames": [
    "slow_tool"
  ],
  "serverCount": 1,
  "serverLog": [
    "recv initialize",
    "send 0 response",
    "recv notifications/initialized",
    "delay tools/list 450",
    "recv tools/list",
    "send 1 response"
  ]
}

$ node --import tsx /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-client.mjs
[bundle-mcp] failed to start server "hangingList" (/root/.nvm/versions/node/v22.22.1/bin/node /tmp/pr85063-hanging-list-explicit-server.mjs): McpError: MCP error -32001: Request timed out
{
  "elapsedMs": 505,
  "toolNames": [],
  "serverCount": 0,
  "serverLog": [
    "recv initialize",
    "send 0 response",
    "recv notifications/initialized",
    "recv tools/list",
    "hang tools/list",
    "recv notifications/cancelled"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: without `toolsListTimeoutMs`, the slow 450 ms `tools/list` response produced `slow_tool` even though the fake server config used `connectionTimeoutMs: 250`; with `toolsListTimeoutMs: 250`, the hanging list request was cancelled and catalog discovery resolved degraded/empty in 505 ms.
- What was not tested: live third-party MCP servers, live SSE/streamable HTTP servers, malformed pagination/page-limit behavior, and full repository test suite. HTTP/SSE config threading is covered by resolver tests; source-runtime proof uses stdio.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before the compatibility fix, the same slow-list source-runtime repro with `connectionTimeoutMs: 250` and no `toolsListTimeoutMs` returned `toolNames: []`, `serverCount: 0`, and logged an SDK request timeout before the fake server's delayed `tools/list` response.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the earlier hardening applied the existing `connectionTimeoutMs` to `client.listTools(...)` after connection, broadening a documented connection-only field into a catalog-list request budget.
- Missing detection / guardrail: coverage had a hanging `tools/list` case, but not the compatibility case where a server connects quickly and returns a slow-but-valid catalog under the SDK default request timeout.
- Contributing context (if known): the existing catalog path already catches per-server listing failures and degrades; the missing piece was separating the post-connect list budget from the connection budget.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts`, `src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: default catalog discovery calls `client.listTools(params)` without a timeout override, an explicit `toolsListTimeoutMs` bounds a hanging list request, and resolver output carries the new optional field for stdio and HTTP transports.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it uses the real session MCP runtime and stdio transport boundary for behavior, plus resolver tests for config threading, while keeping fake servers deterministic and local.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): existing startup/connect tests covered only connection failure/degrade behavior, not post-connect list compatibility.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A; this PR adds regression coverage.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Adds optional `mcp.servers.<name>.toolsListTimeoutMs` for MCP catalog listing. When omitted, OpenClaw preserves the SDK/default request-timeout behavior for `tools/list`; when set, a slow or hung post-connect catalog request is skipped/degraded after that timeout.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Default:
getCatalog() -> connectWithTimeout(connectionTimeoutMs) -> client.listTools(params) -> SDK/default request timeout remains in effect

Opt-in list timeout:
getCatalog() -> connectWithTimeout(connectionTimeoutMs) -> client.listTools(params, { timeout: toolsListTimeoutMs }) -> per-server failure is caught -> partial/empty catalog resolves
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No; this only bounds an existing catalog-listing request and does not execute MCP tools.
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.17.0-14-generic x86_64
- Runtime/container: local source checkout, Node.js v22.22.1
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): bundled MCP stdio transport for source-runtime proof; resolver tests cover stdio and HTTP config threading
- Relevant config (redacted): fake slow-list MCP server with `connectionTimeoutMs: 250` and no `toolsListTimeoutMs`; fake hanging MCP server with `connectionTimeoutMs: 30000` and `toolsListTimeoutMs: 250`

### Steps

1. Run the two source-runtime smoke commands shown in `## Real behavior proof`.
2. Run the focused runtime/config regression tests:
   ```bash
   node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/pi-bundle-mcp-runtime.test.ts src/agents/mcp-transport-config.test.ts
   ```
3. Run related schema/docs/type checks:
   ```bash
   node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.hints.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts
   node scripts/format-docs.mjs --check docs/cli/mcp.md docs/plugins/bundles.md
   node --import tsx scripts/generate-base-config-schema.ts --check
   node --import tsx scripts/generate-config-doc-baseline.ts --check
   node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
   ```

### Expected

- A slow-but-valid `tools/list` response remains visible by default when no list timeout is configured.
- A hanging `tools/list` request degrades quickly when `toolsListTimeoutMs` is configured.
- `connectionTimeoutMs` remains a connection-only budget.

### Actual

- Slow-list source-runtime smoke resolved in 544 ms with `toolNames: ["slow_tool"]` and `serverCount: 1`.
- Explicit-timeout source-runtime smoke resolved in 379 ms with `toolNames: []`, `serverCount: 0`, and `notifications/cancelled` in the fake server log.
- Focused MCP/config tests passed: 4 files / 48 tests.
- Config/schema tests passed: 3 files / 76 tests.
- Docs format, generated config schema checks, generated config baseline check, and core test-project typecheck passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: corrected RED behavior before the fix, slow-but-valid default behavior after the fix, explicit list-timeout degradation after the fix, resolver threading for stdio and HTTP, schema/help/docs/generated-baseline alignment, and core test-project typecheck.
- Edge cases checked: non-positive `toolsListTimeoutMs` is ignored like an absent optional timeout, `connectionTimeoutMs` still defaults to 30 seconds when missing, low `connectionTimeoutMs` no longer shortens `tools/list` by default, and fake server cleanup/disposal paths complete.
- What you did **not** verify: live third-party MCP servers, live non-stdio transports, malformed pagination/page-limit behavior, and full repository test suite.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

The existing compatibility finding is addressed by this code update and proof; no manual bot conversation has been resolved in the GitHub UI.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes; this adds optional `mcp.servers.<name>.toolsListTimeoutMs`.
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. Operators who want a shorter post-connect catalog-listing budget can opt in by setting `toolsListTimeoutMs` per MCP server.

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: a too-low `toolsListTimeoutMs` can still skip a slow-but-valid catalog.
  - Mitigation: the field is opt-in, and the default leaves the SDK/default request timeout in place for compatibility.
- Risk: hung catalogs still wait for the SDK/default request timeout unless the new field is configured.
  - Mitigation: this preserves existing behavior by default and gives operators an explicit list-timeout knob without changing the connection-timeout contract.
- Risk: generated config docs/baseline can drift when adding a new config field.
  - Mitigation: `generate-base-config-schema --check` and `generate-config-doc-baseline --check` passed after regenerating the baseline hash.
